revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:藤森栄一]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1911; {{birth date|1911|8|15}}|death_date=1973; {{death date|1973|12|19}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|1=Empire of Japan}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|archaeologist|qid=Q3621491|1=Archaeologist}}|native_name=藤森栄一|given_name={{ill|Eiichi|qid=Q5349183|1=Eiichi}}}}
'''Eiichi Fujimori''' (藤森 栄一, August 15, 1911 – December 19, 1973) was a Japanese archaeologist, director of the Suwa Archaeological Research Institute, and former president of the Nagano Archaeological Association.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/nandemo/koumoku/1600/160801.htm Suwa City Museum: Nagano Archaeological Association]</ref>
== Early Life and Career == Eiichi Fujimori was born in [[Kami-Suwa]], {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, Japan. He developed an interest in archaeology while attending [[Nagano Prefecture Suwa Seiryo High School]]. Under the guidance of geography teacher Katsue Misawa, he began his studies in archaeology. After graduating in 1929, he moved to Tokyo and joined the Tokyo Archaeological Association, led by [[Rokuji Morimoto]]. Following Morimoto's death, Fujimori collaborated with other notable archaeologists, including [[Sosuke Sugihara]] and [[Yukio Kobayashi]], to manage the association.
In 1936, Fujimori founded Ashikabi Publishing in Tokyo, where he published Morimoto's posthumous manuscript, *The Origins of Japanese Agricultural Culture*. After serving in World War II from 1943 to 1946, he returned to Suwa, where he established a bookstore called Ashikabi and later founded the Suwa Archaeological Research Institute. He conducted numerous excavations focusing on the Suwa region and authored several papers and books on archaeology, folklore, and Suwa Shrine studies.
== Contributions to Archaeology == Fujimori is best known for his "Jomon Agriculture Theory," which he proposed in 1950 based on findings from the [[Idojiri Ruins]] in {{ill|Fujimi, Nagano|en|Fujimi, Nagano|qid=Q1204145|lt=Fujimi, Nagano|1=Fujimi, Nagano}}. He argued that the presence of artifacts like perforated pottery and stone tools in central Japan indicated early agricultural practices during the Jomon period. Unlike the prevailing view of the time, which characterized the Jomon as a purely hunter-gatherer society, Fujimori suggested that they engaged in controlled cultivation of nuts like chestnuts and acorns, as well as early forms of crop cultivation. Although his theory was not widely accepted during his lifetime, subsequent discoveries of cultivated plants and rice remains have led to a re-evaluation of his ideas.
== Legacy == Fujimori mentored many archaeologists, including Atsunori Tozawa, Asei Matsuzawa, and Ken Kirihara. He was instrumental in raising public interest in archaeology through his writings, including *Kamoksha Michi* and *Bronze Bells*, the latter of which won the {{ill|Mainichi Publishing Culture Award|en|Mainichi Publishing Culture Award|qid=Q1554156|lt=Mainichi Publishing Culture Award|1=Mainichi Publishing Culture Award}} in 1964.
He also led conservation efforts to protect the [[Motosu Ruins]] and [[Kiri-ga-mine]] natural areas during the construction of the Venus Line roadway. This movement was later chronicled in the novel *Descendants of the Mist* by [[:en:Jiro Nitta|Jiro Nitta]].
Fujimori passed away in 1973 at the age of 62. The Suwa Archaeological Research Institute continues his work, and the "Eiichi Fujimori Award" was established in his honor to recognize contributions by independent archaeologists.
== Selected Works == * *The Origins of Japanese Agricultural Culture* (1936, editor) * *Kamoksha Michi* (1946) * *Bronze Bells* (1964) * *Idojiri Ruins* (1965) * *Jomon Agriculture* (1970) * *Ancient Paths* (1970, republished in Kodansha Academic Library)
== Posthumous Recognition == Fujimori's collected materials are displayed in the "Eiichi Fujimori Corner" at the Suwa City Museum. In 2023, the museum held an exhibition, "50 Years After His Death: Archaeologist Eiichi Fujimori and Suwa Archaeology," showcasing his contributions to the field.<ref>[https://suwacitymuseum.jp/event/exhibit/6.html Exhibition Details - Suwa City Museum]</ref>
== External Links == * [https://suwacitymuseum.jp/exhibit/ Suwa City Museum]
